html:
Skytech 6937 TW: The PLP Equipment Option Is Real, but Still Mispriced as Noise

Skytech is not the ASIC royalty business in the prior draft. It is a Taiwanese semiconductor equipment company that began in parts, repair, and service, then moved into its own PVD, ALD, bonder/debonder, descum, PECVD, PLP, and EUV inspection tools. The stock’s puzzle is that it screens terribly on 2025 earnings, but the business is in the middle of a product transition where the right number is 2027/2028 earnings power, not trailing EPS.

At the May 15, 2026 close of TWD 293, the market cap is about TWD 19.8 billion, or US$626 million. That is roughly 90x TTM EPS and 8x sales, so this is not a statistically cheap stock. The variant perception is that the 2025 P&L was the wrong denominator. Revenue fell 13% to TWD 2.245 billion and EPS fell to TWD 3.01 because compound semiconductor demand softened and shipments slipped, but the company did not lose the product cycle. It kept gross margin around 40%, continued R&D, and entered 2026 with better order visibility.

The inflection is already visible. Q1 2026 revenue rose 26% to TWD 582 million, operating profit almost tripled to TWD 75.6 million, and EPS rose to TWD 0.96 from TWD 0.73. More important, operating margin moved to 13.0% from 5.9% even though gross margin was flat. January to April revenue was TWD 735.7 million, up 25.5% year over year, and April was still up 22.4%. This is what early operating leverage looks like before the mix shift is obvious.

The PLP tool is the fulcrum. Skytech says it has delivered a 310 x 310 mm panel-level packaging PVD tool for CoPoS-related process needs, with more than 1,000 warped panel transfer tests and more than 300 process stability checks completed before shipment. It has also launched PLP descum into customer validation, while management says OSAT customers are actively engaging. If that becomes a repeat-order platform instead of a single qualification tool, Skytech moves from “small lumpy equipment vendor” to a scarce Taiwan-localized advanced packaging equipment supplier.

That distinction matters because AI packaging is not a normal capex cycle. CoWoS is already the bottleneck investors know. PLP/FOPLP/CoPoS is the next cost and scale experiment as customers push larger substrates, higher density RDL, and more efficient packaging capacity. Skytech does not need to be Applied Materials. It only needs one or two domestically useful tools in a fast-expanding process area where Taiwanese customers prefer nearby customization, fast service, and process co-development.

The market is missing it because the numbers are messy. Equipment revenue recognition is lumpy, the company is small, English disclosure is thin, and 2025 made every linear growth model look wrong. Retail context is also noisy rather than deeply fundamental. CMoney shows thousands of stock-page discussions but little useful current debate, and the visible ETF/fund ownership is tiny. This is still an under-owned local equipment story, not a crowded global AI trade.

The balance sheet gives time but not a hard floor. Q1 assets were TWD 4.52 billion against TWD 1.04 billion of liabilities, with cash roughly offsetting debt and convertible bonds. The risk is inventory, not solvency: inventory was TWD 1.67 billion, nearly 37% of assets. If that converts into shipments, it supports the ramp. If it sits, the thesis is wrong.

The base case is that 2026 revenue exceeds the 2024 record of TWD 2.59 billion, then PLP, ALD, bonding, and service attach lift 2027 revenue toward TWD 3.8 to 4.2 billion. With gross margin in the low-to-mid 40s and opex scaling slower than revenue, EPS can reach TWD 10 to 12. The current price is then 24 to 29x 2027 earnings for a company still early in a domestic advanced packaging equipment cycle.

The bull case is not just more units. It is multi-customer validation. If PLP PVD and descum move from strategic-customer development into OSAT repeat orders, and EUV pellicle inspection or ALD adds a second leg, revenue can push toward TWD 5 billion and EPS can move into the mid-to-high teens. At that point, the market will value Skytech less like a parts-and-service shop and more like a small platform equipment company.

What breaks it is straightforward: May through September revenue stops compounding, Q2/Q3 operating margin falls back below 8%, inventory keeps rising without shipment conversion, PLP descum fails qualification, or management stops talking about OSAT repeat demand. The stock deserves no patience if the next two quarters do not show backlog turning into revenue.

Targets from the current TWD 293 price:

Bear case: TWD 185, down 37%. PLP remains a one-off, 2026 revenue lands near TWD 2.5 to 2.7 billion, EPS stays around TWD 3 to 4, and the stock de-rates as a lumpy small equipment name.

Base case: TWD 540, up 84%. Revenue surpasses TWD 3 billion in 2026, moves toward TWD 4 billion in 2027, EPS reaches TWD 10 to 12, and Skytech earns a premium multiple as a Taiwan-local advanced packaging tool winner.

Bull case: TWD 860, up 194%. PLP PVD and descum become multi-customer platforms, ALD/bonding/EUV inspection add real revenue, EPS reaches TWD 16 to 18, and the stock is re-rated as a scarce advanced packaging equipment platform rather than a cyclical parts vendor.
